previous_statement: (Real vs complex subtlety). For the real PSD cone S^n_+ (real symmetric matrices), the classical BP bound is r(r+1)/2 ≤ m, which is strictly better than r² ≤ m. This is because faces of S^n_+ have dimension r(r+1)/2 (the dimension of the real symmetric r×r matrices), while faces of H_n^+ have dimension r² (the dimension of the Hermitian r×r matrices). Our theorem, working with *-algebras over C, naturally gives the Hermitian/complex version. For real *-algebras (with trivial involution, i.e., A = M_n(R)^{sa}), the commutant analysis would yield π(A)'^{sa} having the dimension of real symmetric matrices, r(r+1)/2, recovering the tighter real bound. The real case requires working with Jordan algebras or real order unit spaces rather than complex *-algebras.
new_statement:
(Real vs complex subtlety — CORRECTED). For the real PSD cone S^n_+ (real symmetric matrices), the classical BP bound is r(r+1)/2 ≤ m, strictly better than r² ≤ m. This arises because faces of S^n_+ have dimension r(r+1)/2 (the dimension of the space of r×r real symmetric matrices), while faces of H_n^+ have dimension r² (the dimension of r×r Hermitian matrices). Our theorem, working with complex *-algebras, naturally gives the Hermitian/complex version.

For the real case, one works with the real *-algebra A = M_n(R) equipped with the TRANSPOSE involution a* = aᵀ (NOT the identity — the transpose is a genuine anti-automorphism). The self-adjoint part A^{sa} = S_n (real symmetric matrices) is the order unit space one optimizes over, but M_n(R) itself is the algebra. The GNS construction for real *-algebras (cf. Goodearl, 'Notes on real and complex C*-algebras'; Li, 'Real operator algebras') produces representations on REAL Hilbert spaces. For A = M_n(R) with a rank-r state (via a rank-r PSD symmetric matrix), the GNS representation gives π ≅ id^{⊕r} on R^n ⊗ R^r, with commutant π(M_n(R))' ≅ M_r(R). The self-adjoint part under transpose is the space of r×r real symmetric matrices, giving dim_R(π(A)'^{sa}) = r(r+1)/2, recovering the tighter real bound.

The full classification by real division algebras (Hurwitz theorem) yields THREE cases: (1) Real: A = M_n(R), involution = transpose, self-adjoint part has dim r(r+1)/2, giving r(r+1)/2 ≤ m+1; (2) Complex: A = M_n(C), involution = conjugate transpose, dim r², giving r² ≤ m+1; (3) Quaternionic: A = M_n(H), involution = conjugate transpose, self-adjoint (self-dual) part has dim r(2r-1), giving r(2r-1) ≤ m+1. All three cases appear in Barvinok's original paper (2001). Our operator-algebraic framework via order unit spaces is well-suited to treat all three cases uniformly through the appropriate choice of *-algebra and involution.
